title: Reading and Use of English – Part 2
description: For questions 9–16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only ONE word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: The Rosetta Stone, discovered in 1799, provided the key (0) to unlocking the mysteries of ancient Egyptian hieroglyphs. For centuries, this complex script (9) had remained undecipherable, its meaning lost to time. The stone features a decree issued in 196 BC (10) on behalf of King Ptolemy V. Crucially, the text appears in three different scripts: hieroglyphic, Demotic, and Ancient Greek. (11) Since the Greek text was well understood, it served (12) as a vital translation tool for researchers. (13) Not until the work of Jean-François Champollion in the 1820s was the code finally broken. Champollion realized that hieroglyphs represented (14) not only symbols but also sounds. This breakthrough allowed historians to read thousands of years of Egyptian history for the first time. (15) Although the stone itself is relatively small, its impact on our understanding of human civilization is immeasurable. Today, it remains (16) one of the most visited artifacts in the British Museum. Visitors from all over the world flock to see this remarkable piece of rock, which bridged the gap between the modern world and an ancient civilization. Its discovery was a turning point that changed archaeology forever and opened doors previously closed forever today.

example.text: The Rosetta Stone, discovered in 1799, provided the key (0) to unlocking the mysteries of ancient Egyptian hieroglyphs.
example.explanation: Preposition following the noun 'key' to indicate purpose or connection.

questions.explanation: Auxiliary verb used to form the past perfect tense.
Part of the fixed prepositional phrase 'on behalf of'.
Conjunction used here to mean 'because' or 'given that'.
Preposition used to describe the function or role of something.
Part of the 'Not until... was' structure used for emphasis with inversion.
Part of the correlative conjunction 'not only... but also'.
Conjunction used to introduce a contrast.
Pronoun used in the structure 'one of the...' to indicate a member of a group.
